name: Jjimdak (Korean Braised Chicken) — Mild
description: Korean Bapsang's Andong-style jjimdak — chicken braised with potatoes, carrot, onion, mushrooms, and sweet-potato glass noodles in a savory-sweet soy braising liquid. A complete one-pot family meal. MADE MILD here by omitting the dried + fresh chili peppers (the source notes you can leave them out entirely).
recipeIngredient: 1 medium chicken, cut up (2.5 to 3 pounds of cut pieces)
2 medium white or yukon gold potatoes (about 10 ounces)
3-4 mushroom caps (rehydrated dried shiitake or fresh)
3-4 tablespoons corn syrup or 2-3 tablespoons honey
OMITTED for mild version: 2-3 dried whole red chili peppers; 1-2 fresh chili peppers or jalapeños
recipeInstructions: Soak the sweet potato starch noodles (dangmyeon) in warm water about 30 minutes while you prep everything else, then drain.
Clean the chicken, cut into small pieces, and trim excess fat.
Cut the potatoes into 1.5-inch chunks and soak in water. Cut the carrot, mushrooms, and onion into bite-size pieces.

Add the chicken to a large pot with 3 cups water and all the sauce ingredients except the sesame oil and sesame seeds. Bring to a boil over high heat, uncovered, about 10 minutes, skimming off the foam.
Add the potatoes, carrot, mushrooms, onion, garlic, and ginger (skip the dried red chilies for a mild version). Cover and cook 8-10 minutes.

Gently mix in the scallions and drained glass noodles (skip the fresh chilies for mild). Cook uncovered 3 more minutes. Stir in the sesame oil and garnish with sesame seeds. Serve from the pot or a large platter.
author: Hyosun / Korean Bapsang

ai.cliffNotes: A one-pot Andong-style braise: chicken simmered with potatoes, carrot, onion, mushrooms and chewy sweet-potato glass noodles in a sweet-savory soy liquid. Complete meal in a single pot — and fully mild when you leave the chilies out.
keyTips: To make it mild (no heat at all), simply omit both the dried red chili peppers and the fresh chilies/jalapeños — the source explicitly says you can leave them out entirely. The dish is still rich and flavorful from the soy-garlic-ginger braise.
Soak the dangmyeon (sweet potato glass noodles) in warm water ~30 min before cooking, and add them only in the last 3 minutes — they finish fast and turn pleasantly chewy without going mushy.
Boil the chicken uncovered for the first ~10 minutes and skim off the foam/scum that rises — that's what keeps the braising liquid clean-tasting and clear.
Soak the cut potatoes in water until they go in; it rinses off surface starch so they hold their shape in the braise instead of falling apart.

Want a deeper mahogany color? Stir in a tablespoon of dark soy sauce (or even instant coffee powder/caramel). Make-ahead: cook through the vegetable stage, then reheat and add noodles + sesame oil just before serving.
Green cabbage, spinach, or bok choy are common add-ins if you want even more vegetables.
ai.source: Claude (prose-mined from the Korean Bapsang article)
